does: RedEdit introduces an automated red-teaming agent that discovers adversarial image edits to evade safety classifiers through a black-box approach. The system combines a Vision-Language-Model proposer that generates semantically targeted candidate edits with Monte Carlo Tree Search planning to explore sequences of photo-editing operations. The method treats image-editing evasion as a combinatorial search problem, avoiding ineffective edit paths through backtracking. Experiments on UnsafeBench demonstrate that fewer than two edits on average enable 76.2% of unsafe images to successfully evade detection while retaining 93.0% of the original malicious semantic content, revealing critical vulnerabilities in deployed safety classifiers.
connects: This work extends adversarial attack research from the pixel-space perturbations and model-specific jailbreaking techniques to the practical domain of user-accessible photo-editing tools. It builds on prior red-teaming frameworks and VLM-based adversarial generation methods while addressing a gap in understanding content moderation robustness. The approach combines recent advances in vision-language models with MCTS planning, a technique previously applied to complex decision-making problems, applied here to iterative image manipulation. By formulating the problem as human-like iterative editing with backtracking, it responds to the limitation of prior work that overlooks realistic attack behaviors in content moderation evaluation.
enables: This research establishes a systematic framework for evaluating and improving safety classifier robustness against practical user-style attacks, informing the design of more resilient content moderation systems. The MCTS-based planning approach could extend to other sequential adversarial domains beyond image editing. Future work can leverage these findings to develop classifiers robust to tool-based manipulations, explore defenses against iterative editing strategies, and extend the framework to video and multimodal content. The methodology also provides a benchmark for assessing classifier vulnerability to incrementally introduced perturbations, supporting the broader goal of building trustworthy AI systems resistant to realistically motivated attacks.
